articles.headline: Government Unveils National Policy on Critical Minerals to Boost Domestic Supply Chains
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the comprehensive National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026, aiming to secure strategic resources for India's industrial growth and green transition.
• The policy identifies 30 critical minerals, including Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, and Rare Earth Elements, crucial for sectors like electric vehicles, renewable energy, and defense.
• Key provisions include incentivizing private sector exploration and mining, establishing a dedicated fund for R&D, and fostering international collaborations for supply chain diversification.
• It introduces a transparent auction mechanism for exploration and mining leases, alongside environmental safeguards and local community engagement.
• The move is expected to reduce India's import dependency, create jobs, and enhance national security by ensuring access to essential raw materials.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
prelimsPoints: National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 approved by Union Cabinet.
Identifies 30 critical minerals.
Focus on reducing import dependency, private sector incentives, R&D fund, international collaboration.
Minerals include Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, Rare Earth Elements.
Sectors impacted: EV, renewable energy, defense.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of critical minerals for India's economic growth and strategic autonomy.
Analyze the key provisions of the National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 and its potential impact on domestic manufacturing and green energy transition.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in implementing a robust critical minerals strategy, including environmental concerns and international cooperation.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of various critical minerals and their applications in modern technology.
articles.trivia: Critical minerals are defined by their economic importance and high risk of supply chain disruption.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflation Concerns, Focus on Growth
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly review, deciding to keep the repo rate unchanged at 6.5% for the sixth consecutive time.
• Governor Shaktikanta Das highlighted persistent inflationary pressures, particularly in food items, as a primary reason for the cautious stance.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the 4% target while supporting economic growth, projecting GDP growth at 7.2% for FY26.
• Analysts suggest the RBI is adopting a 'wait and watch' approach, anticipating global economic shifts and the impact of recent fiscal measures.
• Liquidity management measures were also discussed, with an emphasis on ensuring adequate credit flow to productive sectors.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary policy.
prelimsPoints: RBI MPC maintains repo rate at 6.5%.
Sixth consecutive time for status quo.
Primary concern: food inflation.
Inflation target: 4%.
Projected GDP growth for FY26: 7.2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth, particularly in the context of global uncertainties.
Discuss the effectiveness of current monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflation and its implications for various sectors of the Indian economy.
Evaluate the 'wait and watch' approach of the MPC and suggest alternative or complementary policy measures for sustainable economic stability.
articles.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai with its logo.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members – three officials from the RBI and three external members nominated by the Government of India.

articles.headline: India and Japan Strengthen Strategic Partnership with Enhanced Defence Cooperation Dialogue
articles.explainer:
• The 14th India-Japan Defence Policy Dialogue concluded in Tokyo, reaffirming commitment to a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
• Discussions focused on enhancing maritime security cooperation, particularly in the Indo-Pacific region, through joint exercises and information sharing.
• Both nations explored collaboration in defense technology and equipment, including co-development and co-production opportunities.
• The dialogue also addressed regional security challenges, emphasizing adherence to international law and peaceful resolution of disputes.
• India's Defence Secretary and Japan's Vice Minister of Defence reiterated the importance of a free, open, and rules-based Indo-Pacific.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: 14th India-Japan Defence Policy Dialogue held in Tokyo.
Focus on maritime security in Indo-Pacific.
Collaboration in defense technology and equipment.
Emphasized a free, open, and rules-based Indo-Pacific.
mainsPoints: Examine the evolving dynamics of India-Japan strategic partnership and its significance for regional stability in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss the potential for enhanced defense technology cooperation between India and Japan, considering India's 'Make in India' initiative.
Analyze how India and Japan can collectively address emerging security challenges in the Indo-Pacific, including issues related to freedom of navigation and territorial disputes.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed together.
articles.trivia: Japan is one of India's few partners with whom it holds a '2+2' Ministerial Dialogue (Foreign and Defence Ministers).

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Amendments to Digital India Bill for Robust Data Protection
articles.explainer:
• A Joint Parliamentary Committee (JPC) submitted its report on the proposed Digital India Bill, suggesting several amendments to strengthen data protection and privacy.
• Key recommendations include mandating stronger consent mechanisms, establishing an independent Digital India Authority with adjudicatory powers, and clarifying cross-border data flow regulations.
• The committee emphasized the need for a balanced approach, fostering digital innovation while safeguarding fundamental rights of citizens.
• Concerns were raised regarding the scope of government access to data and the need for clear oversight mechanisms.
• The bill aims to replace the two-decade-old Information Technology Act, 2000, addressing contemporary challenges of the digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Right to Privacy.
prelimsPoints: Joint Parliamentary Committee submitted report on Digital India Bill.
Recommendations focus on data protection and privacy.
Suggested stronger consent mechanisms and independent Digital India Authority.
Aims to replace Information Technology Act, 2000.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the significance of the proposed Digital India Bill in the context of India's digital transformation and the challenges of data governance.
Discuss the key recommendations of the Parliamentary Committee regarding data protection and privacy, evaluating their potential impact on both individuals and digital businesses.
Examine the balance between promoting digital innovation and ensuring robust data protection, considering global best practices and India's unique socio-economic context.
articles.trivia: The Information Technology Act, 2000 was enacted to provide legal recognition for transactions carried out by means of electronic data interchange and other means of electronic communication.

articles.headline: ISRO Provides Update on Shukrayaan-1 Venus Orbiter Mission Progress
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced significant progress on its ambitious Shukrayaan-1 mission, India's first orbiter mission to Venus.
• Scientists confirmed that critical payloads for studying Venus's atmosphere and surface are undergoing final integration and testing phases.
• The mission aims to investigate the planet's atmospheric chemistry, surface features, and interaction with solar wind, offering insights into planetary evolution.
• Shukrayaan-1 is slated for a launch window in late 2026 or early 2027, depending on optimal planetary alignment.
• This mission underscores India's growing capabilities in interplanetary exploration and contributes to global efforts in understanding Venus.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
prelimsPoints: Shukrayaan-1 is ISRO's first Venus orbiter mission.
Aims to study Venus's atmosphere, surface, and solar wind interaction.
Launch window: late 2026/early 2027.
Payloads undergoing final integration and testing.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and scientific significance of ISRO's Shukrayaan-1 mission in advancing our understanding of planetary evolution.
Evaluate India's role and contributions to global space exploration efforts, highlighting the capabilities demonstrated by missions like Shukrayaan-1.
Analyze the technological challenges involved in designing and executing an interplanetary mission to Venus and how ISRO is addressing them.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of the Shukrayaan-1 orbiter orbiting Venus.
articles.trivia: Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to their similar size and mass, but it has a dramatically different atmosphere and surface conditions.

articles.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Shows Promising Progress, Attracting Global Investment
articles.explainer:
• India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is reportedly on track, with several pilot projects achieving operational milestones and attracting significant foreign and domestic investment.
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy highlighted the establishment of new electrolyzer manufacturing units and progress in developing green hydrogen hubs.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization goals.
• Policy incentives, including production-linked incentives (PLI) and infrastructure support, have been instrumental in fostering private sector participation.
• Challenges remain in scaling up production and reducing costs, but the initial phase indicates strong potential for the sector.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission progressing.
New electrolyzer manufacturing units established.
Aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen.
Supported by policy incentives like PLI scheme.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security, climate commitments, and economic growth.
Discuss the key initiatives and policy incentives under the mission that are driving its progress, and identify the major challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production.
Evaluate the potential of green hydrogen to transform various industrial sectors and its role in achieving India's net-zero targets.
articles.imageDescription: Industrial facility producing green hydrogen with wind turbines in the background.
articles.trivia: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable electricity, making it a clean fuel.

articles.headline: Government Initiates Public Consultation for National AI Ethics Framework
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched a public consultation process for India's proposed National AI Ethics Framework.
• The framework aims to establish guidelines for the responsible development and deployment of Artificial Intelligence (AI) across various sectors.
• Key principles include fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, and safety in AI applications, addressing concerns like bias and discrimination.
• Stakeholders from industry, academia, civil society, and the public are invited to provide feedback on the draft document.
• This initiative is part of India's broader strategy to position itself as a global leader in ethical and inclusive AI innovation.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Computers, Robotics, Nano-technology, Bio-technology and issues relating to Intellectual Property Rights.
prelimsPoints: MeitY launched public consultation for National AI Ethics Framework.
Framework aims for responsible AI development and deployment.
Key principles: fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, safety.
Part of India's strategy for ethical AI innovation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges posed by the rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence and the necessity of a comprehensive AI ethics framework for India.
Analyze the proposed principles of India's National AI Ethics Framework, evaluating their potential to mitigate risks like algorithmic bias and ensure equitable access to AI benefits.
Examine the role of multi-stakeholder consultations in shaping effective AI governance policies and compare India's approach with global trends in AI regulation.
articles.imageDescription: Stylized graphic representing AI and ethics symbols.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

articles.headline: Supreme Court to Re-examine Electoral Bonds Scheme Following New Petitions
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court announced it would revisit the constitutionality of the Electoral Bonds scheme, agreeing to hear fresh petitions challenging its transparency and impact on electoral democracy.
• The petitions argue that the scheme, which allows anonymous donations to political parties, undermines the 'right to know' of citizens and facilitates illicit funding.
• A three-judge bench indicated that the matter requires a comprehensive review, especially in light of recent data and ongoing public debate.
• The original challenge to the scheme was referred to a larger bench in 2019, but a final verdict is still pending.
• This development signals renewed judicial scrutiny on electoral financing reforms in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court to re-examine Electoral Bonds scheme.
Petitions challenge transparency and impact on electoral democracy.
Arguments cite undermining 'right to know'.
Original challenge referred to larger bench in 2019.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the Electoral Bonds scheme in India, discussing its stated objectives versus concerns raised regarding transparency and accountability in political funding.
Analyze how the Supreme Court's re-examination of the scheme could impact electoral reforms and the financial landscape of political parties in India.
Discuss the importance of transparency in political funding for a healthy democracy and suggest alternative mechanisms to balance anonymity for donors with public accountability.
articles.trivia: The Electoral Bonds scheme was introduced in the Union Budget 2017-18.

articles.headline: India-Central Asia Dialogue Focuses on Connectivity and Economic Partnerships
articles.explainer:
• The third India-Central Asia Dialogue at the Foreign Ministers' level concluded, emphasizing enhanced connectivity, trade, and cultural ties.
• Discussions focused on activating the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) and Chabahar Port to improve regional connectivity.
• Ministers explored avenues for increasing trade in energy, agriculture, and pharmaceuticals, alongside promoting investment opportunities.
• India reiterated its commitment to capacity building and development assistance for Central Asian nations.
• The dialogue also touched upon regional security issues, including the situation in Afghanistan, and counter-terrorism cooperation.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Third India-Central Asia Dialogue (Foreign Ministers' level).
Focus on INSTC and Chabahar Port for connectivity.
Discussions on trade in energy, agriculture, pharmaceuticals.
Addressed regional security, including Afghanistan and counter-terrorism.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of Central Asia for India's foreign policy and economic interests, particularly in the context of connectivity initiatives.
Discuss how the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) and Chabahar Port can enhance India's trade and geopolitical influence in the Eurasian region.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in deepening its engagement with Central Asian countries, considering regional dynamics and external influences.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the International North-South Transport Corridor.
articles.trivia: The INSTC is a 7,200-km-long multi-modal transport corridor for moving freight between India, Iran, Afghanistan, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Russia, Central Asia and Europe.

articles.headline: BharatNet Phase III to Expedite Rural Broadband Connectivity Across India
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) announced the commencement of BharatNet Phase III, a major push to accelerate broadband connectivity in rural and remote areas.
• This phase aims to connect all remaining gram panchayats and villages with high-speed optical fiber cable (OFC) infrastructure.
• A hybrid model involving both Public-Private Partnership (PPP) and Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF) funding will be utilized for faster deployment.
• The project emphasizes last-mile connectivity and digital inclusion, enabling access to e-governance, e-health, and e-education services.
• Successful completion of BharatNet Phase III is crucial for realizing the vision of Digital India and bridging the urban-rural digital divide.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: BharatNet Phase III launched by DoT.
Aims to connect remaining gram panchayats and villages with OFC.
Utilizes hybrid model: PPP and USOF funding.
Crucial for e-governance, e-health, e-education.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and significance of the BharatNet project in transforming India's rural landscape and fostering digital inclusion.
Analyze the challenges encountered in previous phases of BharatNet and how the strategies for Phase III aim to overcome these hurdles.
Evaluate the socio-economic impact of enhanced broadband connectivity in rural areas, particularly in empowering citizens and promoting local economies.
articles.imageDescription: Optical fiber cables laid across a rural landscape.
articles.trivia: BharatNet is one of the largest rural broadband connectivity projects in the world.

articles.headline: NABARD Launches Special Fund for Climate-Resilient Agriculture Projects
articles.explainer:
• The National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD) announced a new dedicated fund of ₹5,000 crore to support climate-resilient agriculture projects.
• The fund will primarily focus on promoting sustainable farming practices, water conservation, crop diversification, and climate-smart technologies.
• Farmers, Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), and agricultural start-ups will be eligible for financial assistance and technical support.
• This initiative aims to enhance the adaptive capacity of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts and ensure food security.
• The move aligns with India's broader commitment to climate action and achieving sustainable development goals in the agricultural sector.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
prelimsPoints: NABARD launched ₹5,000 crore fund for climate-resilient agriculture.
Focus on sustainable farming, water conservation, crop diversification.
Beneficiaries: farmers, FPOs, agri-start-ups.
Aims to enhance adaptive capacity of agriculture to climate change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the vulnerability of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts and the necessity of promoting climate-resilient practices.
Analyze the role of financial institutions like NABARD in driving sustainable agricultural development and supporting farmers in adopting climate-smart technologies.
Evaluate the potential of dedicated funding mechanisms and FPOs in achieving food security and enhancing farmers' incomes in the face of environmental challenges.
articles.imageDescription: Farmer working in a field with modern agricultural equipment.
articles.trivia: NABARD was established in 1982 as a development bank to focus on the credit needs of agriculture and rural development.

articles.headline: India Calls for Review of Indus Waters Treaty Implementation with Pakistan
articles.explainer:
• India has formally requested Pakistan for a review of certain aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), citing concerns over technical issues and cooperation.
• The Ministry of External Affairs stated that the review is aimed at ensuring the efficient and equitable utilization of shared water resources, as per the treaty's spirit.
• India highlighted Pakistan's persistent objections to Indian hydroelectric projects on western rivers, which India asserts are fully compliant with the IWT.
• This move comes after several rounds of bilateral talks and attempts to resolve disputes through the Permanent Court of Arbitration and a World Bank-appointed Neutral Expert failed to yield a consensus.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank in 1960, governs the sharing of waters of the Indus River system between the two countries.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India requested review of Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan.
Concerns over technical issues and cooperation in implementation.
India highlights Pakistan's objections to Indian hydroelectric projects.
IWT brokered by World Bank in 1960.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and key provisions of the Indus Waters Treaty and its significance for India-Pakistan relations.
Analyze the reasons behind India's call for a review of the treaty's implementation, focusing on the technical and cooperation issues cited.
Evaluate the potential implications of a treaty review on bilateral relations and regional water management, considering the role of international mediation.
articles.imageDescription: Map of the Indus River system showing the distribution of rivers between India and Pakistan.
articles.trivia: Under the Indus Waters Treaty, India has unrestricted use of the three 'eastern rivers' (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej), while Pakistan has unrestricted use of the three 'western rivers' (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).

articles.headline: GST Council Considers Rationalization of Tax Slabs and Compliance Simplification
articles.explainer:
• The GST Council met to discuss potential rationalization of tax slabs and simplification of compliance procedures to enhance ease of doing business.
• A ministerial panel presented recommendations to merge some existing GST rates, particularly aiming to reduce the number of slabs from four to three.
• Focus areas include addressing inverted duty structures, streamlining refund processes, and leveraging technology for better tax administration.
• States raised concerns about revenue implications of slab rationalization and sought assurances on compensation mechanisms.
• The move is aimed at making the Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime more efficient, less complex, and boost overall economic activity.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Taxation.
prelimsPoints: GST Council met to discuss tax slab rationalization and compliance simplification.
Recommendations to merge some GST rates, aiming for three slabs.
Focus on inverted duty structures, refund processes, tech for administration.
States concerned about revenue implications.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and challenges of GST slab rationalization in India, considering its potential impact on revenue, inflation, and businesses.
Analyze the significance of simplifying GST compliance procedures for improving the ease of doing business and fostering economic growth.
Evaluate the role of the GST Council in achieving fiscal federalism and harmonizing indirect taxes in India, highlighting the issues of state autonomy versus national uniformity.
articles.imageDescription: GST logo with Indian currency notes in the background.
articles.trivia: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) was implemented in India on July 1, 2017.

articles.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 Reports Significant Progress in Waste Management
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs released a progress report on Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0, highlighting achievements in solid waste management and sanitation.
• The report indicates a substantial increase in waste processing capacity and segregation at source across urban local bodies (ULBs).
• Focus areas include achieving 'Garbage Free Cities' (GFC) status, remediation of legacy dumpsites, and promotion of circular economy principles in waste management.
• Innovations in waste-to-energy plants and plastic waste management were also showcased.
• The mission aims to make all Indian cities 'water secure' and 'garbage free' by 2026, improving public health and environmental quality.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 progress report released by MoHUA.
Highlights increased waste processing and segregation.
Aims for 'Garbage Free Cities' and legacy dumpsite remediation.
Focus on waste-to-energy and plastic waste management.
Goal: all Indian cities 'water secure' and 'garbage free' by 2026.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and achievements of Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 in transforming urban sanitation and waste management practices in India.
Analyze the challenges encountered in achieving 'Garbage Free Cities' status and suggest innovative solutions for sustainable solid waste management.
Evaluate the role of citizen participation, technological interventions, and policy frameworks in promoting a circular economy approach to waste in urban areas.
articles.imageDescription: Workers segregating waste in an urban setting.
articles.trivia: Swachh Bharat Mission was launched on 2 October 2014, to accelerate the efforts to achieve universal sanitation coverage.

articles.headline: Indian Pharmaceutical Sector Poised for Robust Growth with Focus on R&D and Exports
articles.explainer:
• A new report by the Department of Pharmaceuticals projects significant growth for the Indian pharmaceutical sector, driven by increased R&D investment and strong export performance.
• India is expected to become a major hub for pharmaceutical innovation, particularly in biosimilars, vaccines, and active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs).
• Government initiatives like the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for pharmaceuticals are boosting domestic manufacturing and reducing import dependence.
• Challenges include navigating global regulatory complexities, intellectual property rights issues, and ensuring affordable access to medicines.
• The sector's expansion is crucial for India's healthcare security and its position as the 'pharmacy of the world'.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Investment models.
prelimsPoints: Department of Pharmaceuticals report projects robust growth for Indian pharma sector.
Driven by R&D investment and exports.
India to be hub for biosimilars, vaccines, APIs.
PLI scheme boosting domestic manufacturing.
Challenges: regulatory, IPR, affordable access.
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to the projected growth of the Indian pharmaceutical sector and its strategic importance for national healthcare and global supply chains.
Analyze the impact of government policies like the PLI scheme on boosting domestic pharmaceutical manufacturing and reducing import dependence on Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs).

Examine the challenges and opportunities for India in becoming a global leader in pharmaceutical innovation, particularly in areas like biosimilars and vaccines, while ensuring affordability and accessibility.
articles.trivia: India is the world's largest provider of generic drugs globally, accounting for 20% of global supply by volume.

articles.headline: Ministry Reviews Implementation of Forest Rights Act Amidst Land Dispute Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs conducted a high-level review of the implementation of the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, focusing on accelerating land title distribution and resolving disputes.
• The review acknowledged persistent challenges in recognizing and vesting forest rights, including procedural delays and resistance from some state forest departments.
• The FRA aims to recognize and vest the forest rights and occupation of forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers who have been residing in such forests for generations.
• Emphasis was placed on leveraging technology for efficient claims processing and ensuring the participation of Gram Sabhas in decision-making.
• Proper implementation of FRA is crucial for tribal welfare, forest conservation, and climate action, as tribal communities are often custodians of forests.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Ministry of Tribal Affairs reviewed Forest Rights Act (FRA) 2006 implementation.
Challenges: procedural delays, resistance from forest departments.
FRA recognizes rights of forest dwelling STs and traditional forest dwellers.
Emphasized technology for claims and Gram Sabha participation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and significance of the Forest Rights Act (FRA) 2006 in empowering tribal communities and promoting equitable forest governance.
Analyze the persistent challenges in the effective implementation of FRA, particularly concerning land title distribution and the role of state agencies.
Evaluate how the proper implementation of FRA can contribute to both tribal welfare and sustainable forest conservation efforts in India.
articles.imageDescription: Tribal community members in a forest setting.
articles.trivia: The Forest Rights Act also recognizes 'Community Forest Rights' (CFRs), which allow communities to protect, manage, and conserve their traditional forest lands.

articles.headline: National Urban Flood Management Strategy Under Discussion for Resilient Cities
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Urban Development, in collaboration with NITI Aayog, is finalizing a comprehensive National Urban Flood Management Strategy.
• The strategy aims to enhance the resilience of Indian cities to extreme rainfall events and mitigate the impact of urban flooding.
• Key components include integrated urban planning, upgrading storm water drainage systems, promoting green infrastructure, and early warning systems.
• Emphasis is also placed on community participation and institutional capacity building for effective disaster response.
• This initiative is critical given the increasing frequency and intensity of urban floods due to climate change and rapid urbanization.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: National Urban Flood Management Strategy finalized by MoUD and NITI Aayog.
Aims to enhance city resilience to extreme rainfall and urban flooding.
Components: integrated urban planning, drainage upgrades, green infrastructure, early warning systems.
Critical due to climate change and urbanization.
mainsPoints: Discuss the major causes and consequences of urban flooding in India, linking them to climate change and unplanned urbanization.
Analyze the key features of the proposed National Urban Flood Management Strategy and its potential to build resilient cities.
Evaluate the role of integrated urban planning, green infrastructure, and community participation in developing effective long-term solutions for urban flood mitigation.
articles.imageDescription: Waterlogged urban street during heavy rainfall.
articles.trivia: A 'sponge city' concept, originating in China, aims to manage urban floods by absorbing and reusing rainwater through natural processes.

articles.headline: India Bolsters Maritime Security Strategy with Focus on Indo-Pacific and Blue Economy
articles.explainer:
• India's Ministry of Defence outlined enhancements to its maritime security strategy, emphasizing greater naval presence and multilateral cooperation in the Indo-Pacific.
• The strategy prioritizes safeguarding India's extensive coastline and island territories, securing sea lanes of communication (SLOCs), and protecting maritime economic interests.
• Discussions included increasing indigenous shipbuilding capabilities, modernizing naval assets, and leveraging technology for maritime domain awareness (MDA).
• India also highlighted its commitment to the 'SAGAR' (Security and Growth for All in the Region) vision and promoting a rules-based order in the maritime commons.
• This comprehensive approach aims to counter emerging threats like piracy, maritime terrorism, and illegal fishing, while fostering sustainable blue economy initiatives.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security.
prelimsPoints: MoD outlines enhanced maritime security strategy.
Focus on naval presence and multilateral cooperation in Indo-Pacific.
Priorities: safeguarding coastline, SLOCs, maritime economic interests.
Emphasizes indigenous shipbuilding, naval modernization, MDA tech.
Committed to 'SAGAR' vision and rules-based order.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving maritime security challenges in the Indo-Pacific region and their implications for India's strategic interests.
Discuss the key pillars of India's enhanced maritime security strategy, including its focus on indigenous capabilities, multilateral cooperation, and the 'SAGAR' vision.
Evaluate how a robust maritime security posture contributes to India's blue economy initiatives and its role as a net security provider in the Indian Ocean Region.
articles.imageDescription: Indian Navy warship patrolling in the open sea.
articles.trivia: India has a coastline of over 7,500 kilometers and 1,382 islands, making maritime security a critical aspect of its national defense.

articles.headline: E-Waste Management Rules 2026 Notified, Emphasizing Producer Responsibility
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the new E-Waste Management Rules 2026, replacing previous regulations with stricter provisions.
• The updated rules place greater emphasis on Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), mandating higher collection and recycling targets for manufacturers of electronic goods.
• They introduce a system of online registration and reporting for producers, recyclers, and dismantlers to enhance transparency and traceability.
• The scope of e-waste covered has been expanded to include more categories of electronic and electrical equipment.
• The aim is to promote a circular economy for electronics, reduce environmental pollution from e-waste, and ensure proper disposal and recovery of valuable materials.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: E-Waste Management Rules 2026 notified by MoEFCC.
Replaces previous regulations with stricter provisions.
Greater emphasis on Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) with higher targets.
Introduces online registration and reporting system.
Expanded scope of e-waste covered.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing challenge of e-waste generation in India and its environmental and health implications.
Analyze the key features and innovations introduced in the E-Waste Management Rules 2026, particularly focusing on the concept of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).
Evaluate the effectiveness of regulatory frameworks in promoting a circular economy for electronics and ensuring sustainable e-waste management in India.
articles.trivia: India is the third-largest e-waste producer in the world, after China and the USA.

highlights: Government unveils National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 to secure strategic resources and boost domestic supply chains.
RBI maintains status quo on repo rate at 6.5% for sixth consecutive time, citing persistent inflation concerns.
India and Japan strengthen strategic partnership with enhanced defence cooperation dialogue focusing on Indo-Pacific maritime security.
Parliamentary Panel recommends key amendments to the Digital India Bill for robust data protection and privacy safeguards.
ISRO provides progress update on Shukrayaan-1, India's first Venus orbiter mission, slated for a late 2026/early 2027 launch.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 is/are correct?
1. It aims to reduce India's import dependency on strategic minerals.
2. It identifies 30 critical minerals, including Lithium, Cobalt, and Nickel.
3. It primarily focuses on public sector exploration and mining, with limited private sector involvement.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The policy aims to reduce India's import dependency by boosting domestic supply chains. Statement 2 is correct: It identifies 30 critical minerals, including Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, and Rare Earth Elements. Statement 3 is incorrect: The policy aims to incentivize private sector exploration and mining, not limit it.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of the Reserve Bank of India:
1. The MPC consists of five members, with three from the RBI and two external members.
2. The primary objective of the MPC is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
3. The repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks for short-term needs.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect: The MPC consists of six members – three officials from the RBI and three external members nominated by the Government of India. Statement 2 is correct: The primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth. Statement 3 is correct: The repo rate is indeed the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to India-Japan Defence Cooperation, which of the following areas were discussed in the recent dialogue?
1. Enhancing maritime security in the Indo-Pacific.
2. Collaboration in defense technology and equipment.
3. Joint development of nuclear submarines.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct as the dialogue focused on enhancing maritime security cooperation in the Indo-Pacific and exploring collaboration in defense technology and equipment. Statement 3 is incorrect; there was no mention of joint development of nuclear submarines, which is a highly sensitive area not typically discussed in such dialogues.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following is/are the key principle(s) proposed for India's National AI Ethics Framework?
1. Fairness and Accountability
2. Transparency and Privacy
3. Safety and Security

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: The proposed National AI Ethics Framework aims to establish guidelines based on key principles including fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, and safety in AI applications. Therefore, all three principles mentioned are part of the framework.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT):
1. It was brokered by the United Nations in 1960.
2. India has unrestricted use of the waters of the eastern rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej).
3. Pakistan has unrestricted use of the waters of the western rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect: The Indus Waters Treaty was brokered by the World Bank, not the United Nations. Statements 2 and 3 are correct: The treaty allocates the eastern rivers to India and the western rivers to Pakistan for unrestricted use, with certain provisions for limited use by the other riparian.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the significance of the newly unveiled National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 for India's economic growth, strategic autonomy, and green energy transition. Discuss the challenges and opportunities in ensuring a resilient supply chain for these vital resources. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment. Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc. Investment models.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the policy and its objectives. Discuss its significance for EV, renewable energy, and defense sectors (economic growth, green transition). Explain how it enhances strategic autonomy by reducing import dependence. Detail challenges like exploration costs, environmental concerns, and international competition. Highlight opportunities like private sector investment, R&D, and global partnerships for supply chain resilience. Conclude with its potential for India's future.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's decision to re-examine the Electoral Bonds scheme highlights persistent concerns regarding transparency in political funding. Discuss the arguments for and against the Electoral Bonds scheme and evaluate its impact on electoral democracy in India. Suggest measures to enhance transparency while safeguarding legitimate political contributions. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure. Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act. Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly explain the Electoral Bonds scheme. Present arguments in favor (e.g., curbing black money, formalizing donations) and arguments against (e.g., anonymity, lack of transparency, potential for quid pro quo, 'right to know'). Analyze its impact on electoral democracy (uneven playing field, corporate influence). Suggest measures like capping anonymous donations, mandating disclosure of donors (above a threshold), strengthening the Election Commission's powers, and promoting digital transactions with traceability.

mainsMocks.question: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is poised to transform its energy landscape. Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate commitments. What are the key initiatives under the mission, and what challenges must be addressed to realize its full potential? (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment. Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc. Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Define green hydrogen and its importance for decarbonization and energy security. Highlight India's goals (global hub, net-zero). Mention key initiatives: PLI schemes, green hydrogen hubs, R&D. Discuss challenges such as high production costs, infrastructure development, water availability, and technological advancements. Conclude with the need for sustained policy support and innovation.

quickBytes.text: The National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 identifies 30 critical minerals.
RBI's MPC kept the repo rate unchanged at 6.5% for the sixth consecutive time.
Shukrayaan-1 is ISRO's first orbiter mission to Venus, expected to launch in late 2026/early 2027.
The Digital India Bill aims to replace the two-decade-old Information Technology Act, 2000.
The International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) is a 7,200-km multi-modal route.
NABARD launched a ₹5,000 crore fund for climate-resilient agriculture projects.
The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was brokered by the World Bank in 1960.
The GST Council is considering reducing the number of tax slabs from four to three.
Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 aims for 'Garbage Free Cities' by 2026.
The E-Waste Management Rules 2026 emphasize Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).

GS2.headline: India and Japan Strengthen Strategic Partnership with Enhanced Defence Cooperation Dialogue
GS2.explainer:
• The 14th India-Japan Defence Policy Dialogue concluded in Tokyo, reaffirming commitment to a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
• Discussions focused on enhancing maritime security cooperation, particularly in the Indo-Pacific region, through joint exercises and information sharing.
• Both nations explored collaboration in defense technology and equipment, including co-development and co-production opportunities.
• The dialogue also addressed regional security challenges, emphasizing adherence to international law and peaceful resolution of disputes.
• India's Defence Secretary and Japan's Vice Minister of Defence reiterated the importance of a free, open, and rules-based Indo-Pacific.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: 14th India-Japan Defence Policy Dialogue held in Tokyo.
Focus on maritime security in Indo-Pacific.
Collaboration in defense technology and equipment.
Emphasized a free, open, and rules-based Indo-Pacific.
mainsPoints: Examine the evolving dynamics of India-Japan strategic partnership and its significance for regional stability in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss the potential for enhanced defense technology cooperation between India and Japan, considering India's 'Make in India' initiative.
Analyze how India and Japan can collectively address emerging security challenges in the Indo-Pacific, including issues related to freedom of navigation and territorial disputes.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed together.
GS2.trivia: Japan is one of India's few partners with whom it holds a '2+2' Ministerial Dialogue (Foreign and Defence Ministers).

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Amendments to Digital India Bill for Robust Data Protection
GS2.explainer:
• A Joint Parliamentary Committee (JPC) submitted its report on the proposed Digital India Bill, suggesting several amendments to strengthen data protection and privacy.
• Key recommendations include mandating stronger consent mechanisms, establishing an independent Digital India Authority with adjudicatory powers, and clarifying cross-border data flow regulations.
• The committee emphasized the need for a balanced approach, fostering digital innovation while safeguarding fundamental rights of citizens.
• Concerns were raised regarding the scope of government access to data and the need for clear oversight mechanisms.
• The bill aims to replace the two-decade-old Information Technology Act, 2000, addressing contemporary challenges of the digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies.
Right to Privacy.
prelimsPoints: Joint Parliamentary Committee submitted report on Digital India Bill.
Recommendations focus on data protection and privacy.
Suggested stronger consent mechanisms and independent Digital India Authority.
Aims to replace Information Technology Act, 2000.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the significance of the proposed Digital India Bill in the context of India's digital transformation and the challenges of data governance.
Discuss the key recommendations of the Parliamentary Committee regarding data protection and privacy, evaluating their potential impact on both individuals and digital businesses.
Examine the balance between promoting digital innovation and ensuring robust data protection, considering global best practices and India's unique socio-economic context.
GS2.trivia: The Information Technology Act, 2000 was enacted to provide legal recognition for transactions carried out by means of electronic data interchange and other means of electronic communication.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court to Re-examine Electoral Bonds Scheme Following New Petitions
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court announced it would revisit the constitutionality of the Electoral Bonds scheme, agreeing to hear fresh petitions challenging its transparency and impact on electoral democracy.
• The petitions argue that the scheme, which allows anonymous donations to political parties, undermines the 'right to know' of citizens and facilitates illicit funding.
• A three-judge bench indicated that the matter requires a comprehensive review, especially in light of recent data and ongoing public debate.
• The original challenge to the scheme was referred to a larger bench in 2019, but a final verdict is still pending.
• This development signals renewed judicial scrutiny on electoral financing reforms in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court to re-examine Electoral Bonds scheme.
Petitions challenge transparency and impact on electoral democracy.
Arguments cite undermining 'right to know'.
Original challenge referred to larger bench in 2019.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the Electoral Bonds scheme in India, discussing its stated objectives versus concerns raised regarding transparency and accountability in political funding.
Analyze how the Supreme Court's re-examination of the scheme could impact electoral reforms and the financial landscape of political parties in India.
Discuss the importance of transparency in political funding for a healthy democracy and suggest alternative mechanisms to balance anonymity for donors with public accountability.
GS2.trivia: The Electoral Bonds scheme was introduced in the Union Budget 2017-18.

GS2.headline: India-Central Asia Dialogue Focuses on Connectivity and Economic Partnerships
GS2.explainer:
• The third India-Central Asia Dialogue at the Foreign Ministers' level concluded, emphasizing enhanced connectivity, trade, and cultural ties.
• Discussions focused on activating the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) and Chabahar Port to improve regional connectivity.
• Ministers explored avenues for increasing trade in energy, agriculture, and pharmaceuticals, alongside promoting investment opportunities.
• India reiterated its commitment to capacity building and development assistance for Central Asian nations.
• The dialogue also touched upon regional security issues, including the situation in Afghanistan, and counter-terrorism cooperation.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Third India-Central Asia Dialogue (Foreign Ministers' level).
Focus on INSTC and Chabahar Port for connectivity.
Discussions on trade in energy, agriculture, pharmaceuticals.
Addressed regional security, including Afghanistan and counter-terrorism.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of Central Asia for India's foreign policy and economic interests, particularly in the context of connectivity initiatives.
Discuss how the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) and Chabahar Port can enhance India's trade and geopolitical influence in the Eurasian region.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in deepening its engagement with Central Asian countries, considering regional dynamics and external influences.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the International North-South Transport Corridor.
GS2.trivia: The INSTC is a 7,200-km-long multi-modal transport corridor for moving freight between India, Iran, Afghanistan, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Russia, Central Asia and Europe.

GS2.headline: BharatNet Phase III to Expedite Rural Broadband Connectivity Across India
GS2.explainer:
• The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) announced the commencement of BharatNet Phase III, a major push to accelerate broadband connectivity in rural and remote areas.
• This phase aims to connect all remaining gram panchayats and villages with high-speed optical fiber cable (OFC) infrastructure.
• A hybrid model involving both Public-Private Partnership (PPP) and Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF) funding will be utilized for faster deployment.
• The project emphasizes last-mile connectivity and digital inclusion, enabling access to e-governance, e-health, and e-education services.
• Successful completion of BharatNet Phase III is crucial for realizing the vision of Digital India and bridging the urban-rural digital divide.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: BharatNet Phase III launched by DoT.
Aims to connect remaining gram panchayats and villages with OFC.
Utilizes hybrid model: PPP and USOF funding.
Crucial for e-governance, e-health, e-education.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and significance of the BharatNet project in transforming India's rural landscape and fostering digital inclusion.
Analyze the challenges encountered in previous phases of BharatNet and how the strategies for Phase III aim to overcome these hurdles.
Evaluate the socio-economic impact of enhanced broadband connectivity in rural areas, particularly in empowering citizens and promoting local economies.
GS2.imageDescription: Optical fiber cables laid across a rural landscape.
GS2.trivia: BharatNet is one of the largest rural broadband connectivity projects in the world.

GS2.headline: India Calls for Review of Indus Waters Treaty Implementation with Pakistan
GS2.explainer:
• India has formally requested Pakistan for a review of certain aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), citing concerns over technical issues and cooperation.
• The Ministry of External Affairs stated that the review is aimed at ensuring the efficient and equitable utilization of shared water resources, as per the treaty's spirit.
• India highlighted Pakistan's persistent objections to Indian hydroelectric projects on western rivers, which India asserts are fully compliant with the IWT.
• This move comes after several rounds of bilateral talks and attempts to resolve disputes through the Permanent Court of Arbitration and a World Bank-appointed Neutral Expert failed to yield a consensus.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank in 1960, governs the sharing of waters of the Indus River system between the two countries.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India requested review of Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan.
Concerns over technical issues and cooperation in implementation.
India highlights Pakistan's objections to Indian hydroelectric projects.
IWT brokered by World Bank in 1960.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and key provisions of the Indus Waters Treaty and its significance for India-Pakistan relations.
Analyze the reasons behind India's call for a review of the treaty's implementation, focusing on the technical and cooperation issues cited.
Evaluate the potential implications of a treaty review on bilateral relations and regional water management, considering the role of international mediation.
GS2.imageDescription: Map of the Indus River system showing the distribution of rivers between India and Pakistan.
GS2.trivia: Under the Indus Waters Treaty, India has unrestricted use of the three 'eastern rivers' (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej), while Pakistan has unrestricted use of the three 'western rivers' (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab).

GS2.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 Reports Significant Progress in Waste Management
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs released a progress report on Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0, highlighting achievements in solid waste management and sanitation.
• The report indicates a substantial increase in waste processing capacity and segregation at source across urban local bodies (ULBs).
• Focus areas include achieving 'Garbage Free Cities' (GFC) status, remediation of legacy dumpsites, and promotion of circular economy principles in waste management.
• Innovations in waste-to-energy plants and plastic waste management were also showcased.
• The mission aims to make all Indian cities 'water secure' and 'garbage free' by 2026, improving public health and environmental quality.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 progress report released by MoHUA.
Highlights increased waste processing and segregation.
Aims for 'Garbage Free Cities' and legacy dumpsite remediation.
Focus on waste-to-energy and plastic waste management.
Goal: all Indian cities 'water secure' and 'garbage free' by 2026.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and achievements of Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 in transforming urban sanitation and waste management practices in India.
Analyze the challenges encountered in achieving 'Garbage Free Cities' status and suggest innovative solutions for sustainable solid waste management.
Evaluate the role of citizen participation, technological interventions, and policy frameworks in promoting a circular economy approach to waste in urban areas.
GS2.imageDescription: Workers segregating waste in an urban setting.
GS2.trivia: Swachh Bharat Mission was launched on 2 October 2014, to accelerate the efforts to achieve universal sanitation coverage.

GS2.headline: Ministry Reviews Implementation of Forest Rights Act Amidst Land Dispute Concerns
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs conducted a high-level review of the implementation of the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, focusing on accelerating land title distribution and resolving disputes.
• The review acknowledged persistent challenges in recognizing and vesting forest rights, including procedural delays and resistance from some state forest departments.
• The FRA aims to recognize and vest the forest rights and occupation of forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers who have been residing in such forests for generations.
• Emphasis was placed on leveraging technology for efficient claims processing and ensuring the participation of Gram Sabhas in decision-making.
• Proper implementation of FRA is crucial for tribal welfare, forest conservation, and climate action, as tribal communities are often custodians of forests.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: Ministry of Tribal Affairs reviewed Forest Rights Act (FRA) 2006 implementation.
Challenges: procedural delays, resistance from forest departments.
FRA recognizes rights of forest dwelling STs and traditional forest dwellers.
Emphasized technology for claims and Gram Sabha participation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and significance of the Forest Rights Act (FRA) 2006 in empowering tribal communities and promoting equitable forest governance.
Analyze the persistent challenges in the effective implementation of FRA, particularly concerning land title distribution and the role of state agencies.
Evaluate how the proper implementation of FRA can contribute to both tribal welfare and sustainable forest conservation efforts in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Tribal community members in a forest setting.
GS2.trivia: The Forest Rights Act also recognizes 'Community Forest Rights' (CFRs), which allow communities to protect, manage, and conserve their traditional forest lands.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils National Policy on Critical Minerals to Boost Domestic Supply Chains
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the comprehensive National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026, aiming to secure strategic resources for India's industrial growth and green transition.
• The policy identifies 30 critical minerals, including Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, and Rare Earth Elements, crucial for sectors like electric vehicles, renewable energy, and defense.
• Key provisions include incentivizing private sector exploration and mining, establishing a dedicated fund for R&D, and fostering international collaborations for supply chain diversification.
• It introduces a transparent auction mechanism for exploration and mining leases, alongside environmental safeguards and local community engagement.
• The move is expected to reduce India's import dependency, create jobs, and enhance national security by ensuring access to essential raw materials.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
prelimsPoints: National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 approved by Union Cabinet.
Identifies 30 critical minerals.
Focus on reducing import dependency, private sector incentives, R&D fund, international collaboration.
Minerals include Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel, Rare Earth Elements.
Sectors impacted: EV, renewable energy, defense.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of critical minerals for India's economic growth and strategic autonomy.
Analyze the key provisions of the National Policy on Critical Minerals 2026 and its potential impact on domestic manufacturing and green energy transition.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in implementing a robust critical minerals strategy, including environmental concerns and international cooperation.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of various critical minerals and their applications in modern technology.
GS3.trivia: Critical minerals are defined by their economic importance and high risk of supply chain disruption.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Inflation Concerns, Focus on Growth
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly review, deciding to keep the repo rate unchanged at 6.5% for the sixth consecutive time.
• Governor Shaktikanta Das highlighted persistent inflationary pressures, particularly in food items, as a primary reason for the cautious stance.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the 4% target while supporting economic growth, projecting GDP growth at 7.2% for FY26.
• Analysts suggest the RBI is adopting a 'wait and watch' approach, anticipating global economic shifts and the impact of recent fiscal measures.
• Liquidity management measures were also discussed, with an emphasis on ensuring adequate credit flow to productive sectors.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary policy.
prelimsPoints: RBI MPC maintains repo rate at 6.5%.
Sixth consecutive time for status quo.
Primary concern: food inflation.
Inflation target: 4%.
Projected GDP growth for FY26: 7.2%.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth, particularly in the context of global uncertainties.
Discuss the effectiveness of current monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflation and its implications for various sectors of the Indian economy.
Evaluate the 'wait and watch' approach of the MPC and suggest alternative or complementary policy measures for sustainable economic stability.
GS3.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai with its logo.
GS3.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of six members – three officials from the RBI and three external members nominated by the Government of India.

GS3.headline: ISRO Provides Update on Shukrayaan-1 Venus Orbiter Mission Progress
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced significant progress on its ambitious Shukrayaan-1 mission, India's first orbiter mission to Venus.
• Scientists confirmed that critical payloads for studying Venus's atmosphere and surface are undergoing final integration and testing phases.
• The mission aims to investigate the planet's atmospheric chemistry, surface features, and interaction with solar wind, offering insights into planetary evolution.
• Shukrayaan-1 is slated for a launch window in late 2026 or early 2027, depending on optimal planetary alignment.
• This mission underscores India's growing capabilities in interplanetary exploration and contributes to global efforts in understanding Venus.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
prelimsPoints: Shukrayaan-1 is ISRO's first Venus orbiter mission.
Aims to study Venus's atmosphere, surface, and solar wind interaction.
Launch window: late 2026/early 2027.
Payloads undergoing final integration and testing.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and scientific significance of ISRO's Shukrayaan-1 mission in advancing our understanding of planetary evolution.
Evaluate India's role and contributions to global space exploration efforts, highlighting the capabilities demonstrated by missions like Shukrayaan-1.
Analyze the technological challenges involved in designing and executing an interplanetary mission to Venus and how ISRO is addressing them.
GS3.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of the Shukrayaan-1 orbiter orbiting Venus.
GS3.trivia: Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to their similar size and mass, but it has a dramatically different atmosphere and surface conditions.

GS3.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Shows Promising Progress, Attracting Global Investment
GS3.explainer:
• India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is reportedly on track, with several pilot projects achieving operational milestones and attracting significant foreign and domestic investment.
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy highlighted the establishment of new electrolyzer manufacturing units and progress in developing green hydrogen hubs.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization goals.
• Policy incentives, including production-linked incentives (PLI) and infrastructure support, have been instrumental in fostering private sector participation.
• Challenges remain in scaling up production and reducing costs, but the initial phase indicates strong potential for the sector.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission progressing.
New electrolyzer manufacturing units established.
Aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen.
Supported by policy incentives like PLI scheme.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security, climate commitments, and economic growth.
Discuss the key initiatives and policy incentives under the mission that are driving its progress, and identify the major challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production.
Evaluate the potential of green hydrogen to transform various industrial sectors and its role in achieving India's net-zero targets.
GS3.imageDescription: Industrial facility producing green hydrogen with wind turbines in the background.
GS3.trivia: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable electricity, making it a clean fuel.

GS3.headline: Government Initiates Public Consultation for National AI Ethics Framework
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched a public consultation process for India's proposed National AI Ethics Framework.
• The framework aims to establish guidelines for the responsible development and deployment of Artificial Intelligence (AI) across various sectors.
• Key principles include fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, and safety in AI applications, addressing concerns like bias and discrimination.
• Stakeholders from industry, academia, civil society, and the public are invited to provide feedback on the draft document.
• This initiative is part of India's broader strategy to position itself as a global leader in ethical and inclusive AI innovation.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Computers, Robotics, Nano-technology, Bio-technology and issues relating to Intellectual Property Rights.
prelimsPoints: MeitY launched public consultation for National AI Ethics Framework.
Framework aims for responsible AI development and deployment.
Key principles: fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, safety.
Part of India's strategy for ethical AI innovation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges posed by the rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence and the necessity of a comprehensive AI ethics framework for India.
Analyze the proposed principles of India's National AI Ethics Framework, evaluating their potential to mitigate risks like algorithmic bias and ensure equitable access to AI benefits.
Examine the role of multi-stakeholder consultations in shaping effective AI governance policies and compare India's approach with global trends in AI regulation.
GS3.imageDescription: Stylized graphic representing AI and ethics symbols.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

GS3.headline: NABARD Launches Special Fund for Climate-Resilient Agriculture Projects
GS3.explainer:
• The National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD) announced a new dedicated fund of ₹5,000 crore to support climate-resilient agriculture projects.
• The fund will primarily focus on promoting sustainable farming practices, water conservation, crop diversification, and climate-smart technologies.
• Farmers, Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), and agricultural start-ups will be eligible for financial assistance and technical support.
• This initiative aims to enhance the adaptive capacity of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts and ensure food security.
• The move aligns with India's broader commitment to climate action and achieving sustainable development goals in the agricultural sector.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
prelimsPoints: NABARD launched ₹5,000 crore fund for climate-resilient agriculture.
Focus on sustainable farming, water conservation, crop diversification.
Beneficiaries: farmers, FPOs, agri-start-ups.
Aims to enhance adaptive capacity of agriculture to climate change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the vulnerability of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts and the necessity of promoting climate-resilient practices.
Analyze the role of financial institutions like NABARD in driving sustainable agricultural development and supporting farmers in adopting climate-smart technologies.
Evaluate the potential of dedicated funding mechanisms and FPOs in achieving food security and enhancing farmers' incomes in the face of environmental challenges.
GS3.imageDescription: Farmer working in a field with modern agricultural equipment.
GS3.trivia: NABARD was established in 1982 as a development bank to focus on the credit needs of agriculture and rural development.

GS3.headline: GST Council Considers Rationalization of Tax Slabs and Compliance Simplification
GS3.explainer:
• The GST Council met to discuss potential rationalization of tax slabs and simplification of compliance procedures to enhance ease of doing business.
• A ministerial panel presented recommendations to merge some existing GST rates, particularly aiming to reduce the number of slabs from four to three.
• Focus areas include addressing inverted duty structures, streamlining refund processes, and leveraging technology for better tax administration.
• States raised concerns about revenue implications of slab rationalization and sought assurances on compensation mechanisms.
• The move is aimed at making the Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime more efficient, less complex, and boost overall economic activity.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Taxation.
prelimsPoints: GST Council met to discuss tax slab rationalization and compliance simplification.
Recommendations to merge some GST rates, aiming for three slabs.
Focus on inverted duty structures, refund processes, tech for administration.
States concerned about revenue implications.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and challenges of GST slab rationalization in India, considering its potential impact on revenue, inflation, and businesses.
Analyze the significance of simplifying GST compliance procedures for improving the ease of doing business and fostering economic growth.
Evaluate the role of the GST Council in achieving fiscal federalism and harmonizing indirect taxes in India, highlighting the issues of state autonomy versus national uniformity.
GS3.imageDescription: GST logo with Indian currency notes in the background.
GS3.trivia: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) was implemented in India on July 1, 2017.

GS3.headline: Indian Pharmaceutical Sector Poised for Robust Growth with Focus on R&D and Exports
GS3.explainer:
• A new report by the Department of Pharmaceuticals projects significant growth for the Indian pharmaceutical sector, driven by increased R&D investment and strong export performance.
• India is expected to become a major hub for pharmaceutical innovation, particularly in biosimilars, vaccines, and active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs).
• Government initiatives like the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for pharmaceuticals are boosting domestic manufacturing and reducing import dependence.
